text: Low/moderate-intensity continuous endurance training, high-intensity interval training, and sprint interval training increase mitochondrial content to a similar extent (ET 23±5%, HIT 27±5%, SIT 27±7%) when adjusted for training frequency, intervention duration, and initial fitness level.
population: mixed (5973 participants across fitness levels, ages, and health status)

text: Per total hour of exercise, sprint interval training is approximately 3.9 times more efficient than endurance training and 2.3 times more efficient than high-intensity interval training for increasing mitochondrial content.
specific_value: SIT ~3.9x more efficient than ET, ~2.3x more efficient than HIT per hour

text: Higher training frequencies (6 > 4 > 2 sessions per week) are associated with larger increases in both mitochondrial content and VO2max.

Capillary density (capillaries per mm²) only increases after endurance training (13±3%) and high-intensity interval training (7±4%), with endurance training producing significantly greater gains than HIT or SIT.
Gains in capillarization occur primarily in the early stages of training (less than 4 weeks) and are only observed in untrained to moderately trained participants.
Lower initial fitness levels are associated with greater percentage improvements in mitochondrial content, capillarization, and VO2max in response to exercise training.
The ability to adapt mitochondrial content and capillarization to exercise training is maintained throughout life, irrespective of age, sex, menopause, or presence of disease.
population: general population including older adults and those with chronic disease

text: Women display greater percentage gains in VO2max compared with men in response to exercise training.
